sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage: Investigators studied oxygen delivery in maternal and fetal blood samples. Adult hemoglobin was tested at normal pH and after lactic acid was added to mimic metabolically active tissue. Fetal hemoglobin was tested under the same oxygen tensions. The oxygen-saturation curves are shown in the figure. The investigators concluded that local tissue metabolism and fetal hemoglobin structure both alter oxygen unloading without changing the amount of heme per tetramer.
questions.stem: Which conclusion is best supported by the curve for adult hemoglobin at lower pH?

choices.A: Lower pH decreases hemoglobin affinity for oxygen, promoting oxygen unloading in active tissues.
choices.B: Lower pH increases hemoglobin affinity for oxygen in active tissues.
choices.C: Lower pH prevents oxygen from binding to the heme iron atom.
choices.D: Lower pH converts hemoglobin from a tetramer to a monomer.

explanations.A: Correct. The rightward shift at pH 7.2 is the Bohr effect and helps deliver O2 where CO2 and H+ are high.
explanations.B: The curve shifts right, not left, so affinity decreases.
explanations.C: Oxygen still binds; the change is in affinity, not complete loss of binding.
explanations.D: The passage states that heme content per tetramer is unchanged, and Bohr effect does not require dissociation.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Hemoglobin oxygen saturation as a function of oxygen tension under three conditions.

questions.stem: Which molecular interaction most directly explains fetal hemoglobin having higher O2 affinity than adult hemoglobin?

choices.A: Fetal hemoglobin contains iron in the Fe3+ state.
choices.B: Fetal hemoglobin binds 2,3-BPG less strongly than adult hemoglobin.
choices.D: Fetal hemoglobin hydrolyzes CO2 into bicarbonate more rapidly.

explanations.A: Fe3+ methemoglobin would not bind oxygen normally.
explanations.B: Correct. Reduced 2,3-BPG binding stabilizes the R state, shifting the curve left and allowing fetal blood to load O2 from maternal blood.
explanations.C: Functional fetal hemoglobin is still tetrameric.
explanations.D: Carbonic anhydrase chemistry is not the direct basis of the fetal Hb curve shift.

questions.stem: At PO2 = 40 mmHg, which condition would deliver the largest fraction of bound oxygen to tissue compared with saturation at 100 mmHg?

choices.D: All three deliver the same amount because PO2 is the only determinant.

explanations.A: Fetal hemoglobin remains highly saturated at 40 mmHg, so it unloads less oxygen.
explanations.B: It unloads oxygen, but less than the low-pH condition.
explanations.C: Correct. Adult Hb at low pH has the lowest saturation at 40 mmHg while remaining nearly saturated at 100 mmHg, maximizing unloading.
explanations.D: The figure shows that hemoglobin condition changes saturation at the same PO2.

questions.stem: A high concentration of CO2 in peripheral tissue would most likely produce which additional effect?

choices.A: Decreased H+ concentration inside red blood cells
choices.B: Direct conversion of heme iron from Fe2+ to Fe3+
choices.C: Inhibition of chloride shift into red blood cells
choices.D: Increased bicarbonate formation in red blood cells through carbonic anhydrase activity

explanations.B: CO2 transport does not normally oxidize heme iron.
explanations.C: Bicarbonate export is coupled to chloride entry to maintain charge balance.
explanations.D: Correct. CO2 enters RBCs and is hydrated to carbonic acid, which dissociates to bicarbonate and H+, contributing to the Bohr effect.

questions.stem: Which change would most likely shift the adult hemoglobin curve in the same direction as low pH?

explanations.A: Correct. 2,3-BPG stabilizes the T state of adult hemoglobin, lowering O2 affinity and shifting the curve right.
explanations.B: Lower temperature generally shifts hemoglobin left.
explanations.C: Less CO2 reduces the Bohr effect and shifts left.
explanations.D: Fetal chains reduce 2,3-BPG binding and shift left.

questions.passage: A second group measured cardiac function in isolated mammalian hearts exposed to Drug B. Drug B reduced heart rate and increased end-diastolic volume. End-systolic volume also increased. Contractile protein ATP levels were unchanged, but phosphorylation of L-type Ca2+ channels decreased. The figure summarizes the main measurements.
questions.stem: Based on the figure, what happened to stroke volume after Drug B treatment?

choices.A: It decreased from 120 mL to 65 mL.
choices.B: It slightly increased from 70 mL to 75 mL.
choices.C: It remained exactly unchanged at 70 mL.
choices.D: It became equal to heart rate divided by EDV.

explanations.A: That subtracts values across the wrong categories and does not calculate stroke volume.
explanations.B: Correct. Stroke volume equals EDV minus ESV. Control SV = 120 − 50 = 70 mL; Drug B SV = 140 − 65 = 75 mL, so it slightly increased.
explanations.D: Stroke volume is EDV − ESV, not HR/EDV.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Cardiac volumes and heart rate before and after Drug B.

questions.stem: Which effect of Drug B best explains the reduced phosphorylation of L-type Ca2+ channels?

choices.B: Increased aldosterone secretion at the myocardium
choices.D: Activation of skeletal muscle nicotinic receptors

explanations.A: Parasympathetic cardiac signaling primarily uses acetylcholine, not norepinephrine.
explanations.B: Aldosterone regulates renal sodium handling more than acute L-type Ca2+ channel phosphorylation.
explanations.C: Correct. β1 stimulation normally increases cAMP/PKA activity, which phosphorylates Ca2+ channels and increases contractility and heart rate.
explanations.D: Nicotinic receptors at the neuromuscular junction do not explain cardiac L-type channel phosphorylation.

questions.stem: Which variable would be most directly reduced if Drug B lowered contractility while EDV stayed constant?

explanations.A: Venous return determines preload but is not the direct ratio affected by contractility at fixed EDV.
explanations.B: Osmolarity is unrelated to acute ventricular contractility.
explanations.C: Cardiac contractility does not directly determine hemoglobin oxygen affinity.
explanations.D: Correct. Lower contractility increases ESV relative to EDV, decreasing SV/EDV, which is ejection fraction.

questions.stem: If Drug B increased preload, which mechanism would normally help preserve stroke volume?

choices.A: Greater ventricular stretch increasing actin-myosin overlap within an optimal range
choices.B: Opening of voltage-gated Na+ channels in erythrocytes
choices.C: Reduced sarcomere length below optimal overlap
choices.D: Complete inhibition of Ca2+ release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum

explanations.A: Correct. The Frank-Starling mechanism links increased EDV to increased force of contraction through length-dependent tension.
explanations.B: RBCs do not generate action potentials for ventricular contraction.
explanations.C: Reducing length would generally reduce force.
explanations.D: Blocking Ca2+ release would impair contraction rather than preserve stroke volume.

questions.stem: Which calculation gives cardiac output under control conditions?

explanations.A: Volumes are not multiplied together to calculate cardiac output.
explanations.B: Correct. Cardiac output equals stroke volume times heart rate, and stroke volume equals EDV minus ESV.
explanations.C: This inverts the relationship; cardiac output is SV × HR.
explanations.D: EDV and ESV are not added, and heart rate should multiply SV.
